description: A single rasterized triangle on screen, illustrating the concept of triangle rasterization in traditional computer graphics as an analogy for Gaussian splatting.
teaching_value: Anchors the new concept of Gaussian splatting to the familiar concept of triangle rasterization, helping students understand that both are rasterization techniques differing only in primitive type.
when_to_show: Show before introducing Gaussian splatting, when establishing the analogy between triangle rasterization and Gaussian rasterization.
suggested_caption: Triangle rasterization: the classical primitive analogous to Gaussian splatting. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: A single 2D Gaussian splat rendered on screen with a visible border outline, showing its elliptical shape and soft opacity falloff from center to edge.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of what one Gaussian primitive looks like when rasterized, making abstract mathematical descriptions tangible and showing key properties like shape and opacity gradient.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the Gaussian as the rendering primitive, before discussing its parameters, to ground the parameter discussion in a visible example.
suggested_caption: A single rasterized Gaussian with border — the core rendering primitive. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: Three overlapping 2D Gaussian splats rendered together on screen, demonstrating how multiple Gaussians composite to form a combined image.
teaching_value: Shows students how individual Gaussians combine through alpha compositing, illustrating that the final scene is built by layering many such primitives — a key step toward understanding large-scale scenes.
when_to_show: Show after the single Gaussian image, when scaling up from one primitive to many, to bridge the gap between a single splat and a full scene.
suggested_caption: Three composited Gaussians — building blocks of a full 3DGS scene. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: A photorealistic rendered image of a bicycle scene reconstructed using approximately 7 million fully opaque Gaussian splats, showing fine detail and scene complexity.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical visual quality achievable with 3D Gaussian Splatting at scale, motivating why the technique is powerful and showing the end goal of the training procedure.
when_to_show: Show when motivating the technique's quality and scale, after explaining individual Gaussians, to illustrate what millions of Gaussians can reconstruct.
suggested_caption: 7 million fully opaque Gaussians reconstruct a photorealistic bicycle scene. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: A 3D visualization of ellipsoids in space, representing 3D Gaussians before projection, showing their varying sizes, orientations, and positions in the scene.
teaching_value: Helps students visualize the 3D nature of Gaussian primitives — their covariance-defined ellipsoidal shape in 3D space — before they are projected (splatted) onto the 2D image plane.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the 3D-to-2D projection step, to clarify that Gaussians exist as 3D ellipsoids that are then splatted onto the screen.
suggested_caption: 3D Gaussian ellipsoids in space before projection onto the image plane. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)

description: A sparse 3D point cloud of a scene, as produced by Structure from Motion (SfM) using COLMAP, showing scattered colored points representing estimated 3D positions.
teaching_value: Illustrates the initialization step of 3DGS training — students can see that the process starts from a sparse, noisy SfM point cloud, and each point becomes the seed for a Gaussian, clarifying why training is needed to improve quality.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the training pipeline explanation, when describing how SfM output is converted into initial Gaussians before optimization begins.
suggested_caption: SfM point cloud from COLMAP — the initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting training. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
